s1.headline: "I need to think about it" is not an objection.
s1.body: It's something that broke before the call. A 45-day sprint for founders patching the pitch instead of fixing the offer, the channel, and the ICP

s2.headline: The prospect leaves informed, not convinced.

quotes.q: "Let me discuss this with my partner."
quotes.a: — but you don't hear from them.

quotes.q: "Can you send me a proposal so I'll get back to you."
"Sounds good but the timing isn't right."

quotes.a: — and it's Q1 next year, and still circling.

s3.headline: Five places the sale broke before the call.

rows.q: You have one ICP on paper. But you smuggled 3 new ones in the last 6 months.
rows.a: You had the workshop. You nailed the ICP. Then a referral came in with a different problem and you signed it. Then one asked for events. Another for Meta ads. Each looked like a defensible exception at the time. Six months of that and the offer bends in three directions, the site speaks to everyone, and the right-fit prospect reads it and can't see themselves.

rows.q: The site lists every service you've ever sold. Your ideal buyer needs only 1.
rows.a: You keep every service on the menu because removing one feels like closing a revenue door. But your client needs to feel you are the expert on their specific problem. When they scan your offer, they can't find what they came to solve. It reads like a restaurant menu. Complete from your side. Unclear from theirs.

rows.q: Outreach feels icky. Or you're messaging everyone.
rows.a: You avoid cold outreach because you don't want to be salesy, or you blast it to everyone including the gardener. The right conversation with the right prospect opens an opportunity that solves their problem and creates pipeline for yours.

rows.q: Inconsistent posts that don't turn into meetings.
rows.a: You post on LinkedIn. Sometimes you get likes. The DMs from prospects never come. You optimize content for the algorithm and teach the algorithm, not the buyer. A post earns the lead when it names a specific moment the reader already lives inside, not when it condenses five principles into a carousel.

rows.q: Sales calls that end in 'I need to think about it.'
rows.a: The call felt good. They followed along, said it was useful, asked for pricing. Then nothing. You replay the conversation looking for what went wrong. Nothing went wrong on the call. The deal broke earlier, in the ICP, the offer, the cold outreach, or the positioning that brought the wrong version of the right prospect through the door.

s4.headline: The work sits in three places.

cols.body: Who you're for isn't an aspiration. It's a pattern in the roster — the deals that closed fast, paid on time, and referred others. The work is pulling that pattern out and making it the filter for everything that follows.
fixes: "You have one ICP on paper. Three others in your pipeline."

cols.body: Everything a prospect reads before the call. Site, LinkedIn, outreach, content. When the offer is one specific thing, the reader can repeat it back to a colleague. When it's twenty-seven things, they forget what you said by the time the call ends.
fixes: "The site lists every service you've ever sold. Your ideal buyer needs only 1."
"Outreach that opens with what you do."

cols.body: The call isn't a script problem; it's a structure problem. Rebuilt, it surfaces fit early and isolates the real objection. The right-fit prospect signs instead of stalling. The wrong-fit one disqualifies before the quote, not after.
fixes: "Sales calls that end in 'I need to think about it.'"

s6.headline: Nothing in the pipeline moves on its own.
s6.payoff: Every sales call rewrites what came before it.

s6Anchor.text: See 21 days of this run end-to-end →

s7.headline: Twenty-one days. Three weeks, three places.
s7.body1: Week one: who you're for. Week two: what you say. Week three: how you sell. You bring the current state — site, offer, outreach, call recordings. We rebuild the parts making the sale harder than it needs to be.
s7.body2: First three pilots at €4K in exchange for case-study rights. After that, €7.5K–10K depending on scope.

s8.founderHeadline: I spent ten years doing this at scale.
s8.founderBody1: CRM and customer journeys at adidas. Commercial strategy at Heineken. Enterprise sales at DELL. The difference now is I do it for founders who sell expertise, not servers, sneakers, or beer.

faq.q: How is this different from a productized service.
faq.a: A productized service sells a repeatable deliverable. The sprint rebuilds three connected things — the ICP, every surface the offer shows up on, and the sales call structure. The deliverable is a working conversion path, not a document.

faq.q: What you need from me during the twenty-one days.
faq.a: Two hours of founder time per week, across three working sessions. Read-access to the last ten sales call recordings. Edit-access to the site and LinkedIn. Everything outside those inputs happens on our side.

faq.q: What happens if my ICP is wrong.
faq.a: Week one is built to catch that. If the ICP we surface in the roster pattern doesn't match who you thought you were selling to, week two rebuilds the surfaces around the real one. You don't leave with a rewritten site pointing at the wrong buyer.

faq.q: Whether this works if my pipeline is agency, consulting, or training.
faq.a: The sprint works on any B2B service business where the sale runs through a call and the prospect can stall at "I need to think about it." Agency, consulting, advisory, training. What doesn't fit: e-commerce, SaaS self-serve, or anything that closes without a conversation.

You own what we built. Site, offer, outreach copy, call structure — all yours, all documented, all repeatable by you or whoever picks this up next. No retainer, no upsell, no dependency. If the sale starts stalling again six months later, you'll know where to look.

s8.payoff: If you've read this far, the next move is a fifteen-minute call.
